flashcards.front: What is the primary purpose of the Requirements Model in software engineering?
flashcards.back: The requirements model (or analysis model) serves as a bridge between the problem domain and the solution space. It transforms stakeholder needs, expectations, and organizational constraints into a structured, unambiguous representation of the system to be developed, capturing both functional requirements and quality attributes.

flashcards.front: Define Analysis Modeling and list its primary components.
flashcards.back: Analysis Modeling is a systematic approach that creates abstract descriptions of the system from the user's perspective. Its primary components include: (1) Scenario-based modeling (use cases), (2) Class-based modeling (object analysis), (3) Behavioral modeling (state/sequence diagrams), (4) Flow-oriented modeling (data flow diagrams), and (5) Data modeling (ER diagrams).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Aggregation and Composition in class-based modeling?
flashcards.back: Aggregation represents a 'has-a' relationship where the part can exist independently of the whole (e.g., Department has Teachers). Composition is a stronger 'has-a' relationship where the part cannot exist without the whole (e.g., Book has Pages - pages cannot exist without the book). In composition, when the whole is destroyed, all parts are destroyed.

flashcards.front: Explain the concept of multiplicity in data modeling with examples.
flashcards.back: Multiplicity defines the number of instances that can participate in a relationship. Examples: (1) One-to-One (1..1): One Student has One ID Card, (2) One-to-Many (1..*): One Department has Many Employees, (3) Many-to-Many (*..*): Students enroll in Many Courses, and each Course has Many Students. Multiplicity is represented as (min..max) at each end of an association.

flashcards.front: What are the key elements of a Use Case?
flashcards.back: A Use Case includes: (1) Actor - external entity interacting with the system, (2) Use Case Name - specific function or service, (3) Preconditions - what must be true before execution, (4) Postconditions - what must be true after successful completion, (5) Basic Flow - primary successful scenario, (6) Alternative Flows - alternate or error scenarios, (7) Business Rules - constraints governing the use case.

flashcards.front: Define Requirements Traceability and explain its importance.
flashcards.back: Requirements Traceability is the ability to track requirements throughout the software development lifecycle. Forward traceability maps requirements to design, code, and test artifacts; backward traceability maps design/code back to source requirements. It ensures all requirements are implemented, helps identify impact of changes, and is crucial for validation and regulatory compliance (e.g., FDA, FAA).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Functional and Non-Functional requirements?
flashcards.back: Functional requirements define specific behaviors/functions the system must perform (e.g., 'User shall be able to search products'). Non-Functional requirements (quality attributes) specify system characteristics like performance, security, usability, reliability, and maintainability. Non-functional requirements often constrain the functional ones.

flashcards.front: Explain the concept of CRC Cards in class-based modeling.
flashcards.back: CRC (Class-Responsibility-Collaboration) Cards are index cards used for object-oriented analysis. Each card has: (1) Class Name, (2) Responsibilities - what the class knows and does, (3) Collaborators - other classes needed to fulfill responsibilities. CRC cards facilitate team discussion and help identify classes, their roles, and relationships during early analysis.

flashcards.front: What is a State Diagram and what does it model?
flashcards.back: A State Diagram is a behavioral model that shows the discrete states an object or system can occupy and the transitions between those states. It consists of: (1) States (circles), (2) Transitions (arrows with events), (3) Initial/Final states, (4) Guard conditions. Used to model dynamic behavior of reactive systems, like order processing, login flows, or ATM operations.

flashcards.front: Define the concept of 'Inheritance' in object-oriented analysis modeling.
flashcards.back: Inheritance (or generalization) is a relationship where a subclass inherits attributes and operations from a parent/superclass. It represents an 'is-a' relationship (e.g., 'SavingsAccount is a BankAccount'). Benefits include: code reuse, reduced redundancy, easier maintenance, and natural taxonomy creation. The subclass may override inherited behavior or add new capabilities.

flashcards.front: What are the elements of a Data Flow Diagram (DFD)?
flashcards.back: A DFD consists of four elements: (1) Processes (circles/rounded rectangles) - transformations on data, (2) Data Stores (open-ended rectangles) - repositories of data, (3) External Entities (squares) - sources/sinks outside the system, (4) Data Flows (arrows) - pathways for data movement. DFDs use decomposition to show varying levels of detail.

flashcards.front: What is Requirements Validation and what techniques are used?
flashcards.back: Requirements Validation ensures that requirements accurately represent stakeholder needs and are free from defects. Techniques include: (1) Requirements Reviews (formal/informal), (2) Prototyping, (3) Test Case Generation, (4) Consistency Checking, (5) Feasibility Analysis, (6) Acceptance Criteria Definition. Validation differs from verification (checking conformance to specifications) - validation ensures we're building the right product.
